task_lines: Generate a detailed chapter-by-chapter romantic comedy novella plan.
Return exactly 15 chapters.
Return chapter details that deepen the existing beats and maintain novella pacing.
Treat the 15-chapter structure as fixed unless the user explicitly changes it later.
Aim for roughly 3,000 words per chapter as the planning target, while recognizing that drafted chapters may often land closer to 2,000 words.
Design the plan so the complete short novel still works well in an actual range of roughly 30,000 to 45,000 words.

Assign each chapter its formula_beat from the romance Formula, in order: Meet-Cute (ch 1), First Refusal (ch 1-2), The Problem Sticks Them Together (ch 2), Slow Burn (ch 3-5), What If This Could Work (ch 6), The Declaration (ch 7-8), The New Baseline (ch 9-10), The Blow-Up (ch 11), It's So Over (ch 12-13), We're So Back (ch 14), The Deal Is Sealed + Happily Ever After (ch 15).
Chapter 1's meet-cute is the scene readers will reread most: plan it to be memorable, and plant the hint of why these two are perfect for each other.

During the Slow Burn chapters, challenge each lead's preconceptions about the other and stack counterarguments against the first refusal reason, while showing the full scale of the gap that keeps them apart.
In The New Baseline chapters, plant and grow a visible crack: a threat, fear, or pressure that builds until the Blow-Up. It must be telegraphed, never sprung.

The Blow-Up must not be caused by an avoidable misunderstanding. Prefer an external threat tied to the central pressure; if the cause is intrinsic, it must have been planted early, and there must be a reinforced reason the leads cannot resolve it by talking like adults.
In the It's So Over chapters, test the bond honestly but never make either lead act unreasonably or immaturely, and never erase relationship development they have earned.

In We're So Back, the leads' love must be a key element of overcoming the threat: a declaration against all odds, an intervention at the critical moment, or the two of them in perfect sync against the problem.

Chapter 15 delivers the earned catharsis at full strength (the big kiss, the wedding, the public claiming, sized to the heat level) and closes with a happily-ever-after glimpse: joyful, safe, together. Pull no punches on the payoff.
Keep the output as chapter architecture and scene-planning bones, not draftable prose.
Each chapter entry should be concrete enough that a writer or downstream AI can draft from it later.
Carry one dominant story lane through the chapter plan: one main romance arc, one central external pressure, and one emotional question.
State the POV owner for each chapter explicitly.
Each chapter must obey the scene contract: goal, friction or interruption, meaningful change, emotional beat, and carry-forward thread.
Escalate the relationship in earned stages rather than looping or jumping ahead.
Keep both leads active co-protagonists throughout: the love interest must never fade into background noise, especially after the Declaration.
If supporting cast is provided, integrate them into the chapter plan as active forces that change scenes, not just mentioned names.
Every major supporting character should alter at least one chapter's conflict, reveal, power balance, or emotional turn.
Set each approximate_word_target near 3000 unless a specific chapter benefits from being modestly shorter or longer.
